author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text: It might be useful for people to look into how they bundle bamboo together in modern architecture, and using organic curves and such.   Strong and really cool aesthetically, and you can use thinner bamboo for structural elements.  A lot of the river cane around here is about 1/2" diameter or less, so that would make it viable for projects where it's not immediately apparent that it is.

author: @justinw1765
text:
No, NOT carbon fiber (ever).  Very different expansion/contraction than bamboo!  Will eventually crack it (especially with any extreme temp or humidity/moisture changes).  

   A cool tip, though not the most "eco", is to knock out the internal culms some, and then spray expanding foam into the bamboo.  It makes the structure stiffer and absorbs even more vibration, and also helps to adhere things internally some (besides the outer lashings/wrappings).

  Bamboo is even better when all of it is reinforced with fiberglass--preferably S-grade type fiberglass, and either epoxy or Titebond III wood glue.  Add some carbonized nanocellulose crystals (like natures version of carbon nanotubes) in with the epoxy or Titebond III (at around 2% by weight to the "glue" matrix) and combine it with specifically with S-glass fiberglass (a couple wraps of say 4 oz/yd2 fabric), and you got a structure that is tougher than a carbon fiber tube. Similar tensile strength at lower weight, but better impact resistance. Does not have quite as high Young's Modulus strength ("stiffness" strength), but not far behind.  For best strength to weight ratios though, use high quality composite specific type epoxy (more $$ though).  These typically range from around 7k to 12k psi tensile strength, vs small quantity, home improvement store type epoxies that range from about 3k to 6k psi tensile strength--quite a difference!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: really? Carbon fibre is used to build all the high end bamboo bikes you see - I am not sure you are right in what you say there - nice tip on the foam though - cheers

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering   Sorry I'm just getting back to this.  One of the oldest bamboo making companies around, Calfee, first used carbon fiber to wrap the joints but found that these had too much problems with cracking/splitting the bamboo after awhile, and they figured out it was due to the high difference in expansion and contraction rates between the different materials.  

    That is how I learned about it, from a company that learned the hard way.  

  If you actually look up the properties of carbon fiber, you'll see that when it gets warm, it actually slightly contracts/shrinks! And CF is not affected by humidity, or so little to not be noticeable. Very different than bamboo, which when it gets warm and/or saturates with high humidity, will somewhat significantly expand.  

   So if you have this super high tensile strength, and very high stiffness material overlaid onto the bamboo, pretty much like a vice, when you go from temperature and/or humidity extremes, what do you think is going to happen?  If the bamboo starts to expand, and the carbon fiber starts to slightly contract/shrink at the same time, what is going to give?  Not the carbon fiber (having a significantly higher tensile and Young's modulus strength), but it certainly can and will eventually crack/split the bamboo.  If you kept it inside a climate controlled building all the time, it would be fine, but then again that would be kind of pointless..

    I suppose this could be offset/counter balanced some by wrapping less tightly and/or using a specialized epoxy like G Flex which is more tough and yet flexible than many other epoxies.  But why go to the trouble when fiberglass, and to a lesser extent some natural fibers, work pretty well at lower cost both financially and environmentally?

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: I am making small windmills for children with bird feathers, cork and bamboo. My experience is that bamboo doesn’t wear out and can be used as a Bush bearing. Try it out! I used BBQ sticks for the axle, and hollow garden bamboo sticks for the bearings. Drilled holes in the corks and without glue everything can be made fit together. After a couple of months and severe storms I change the feathers, but my basic constructions have lasted for more than 10 years!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: wow - very nice tip mate - I'll try it

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Awesome using natural resources. Pity we live in a throw away society.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Agreed. However, buying it from a place that buys it from China is not the most eco/sustainable option either. Better to get it locally if one can, though I suppose that is probably hard, if not impossible to do in England?   I live in the south east of the US, and the stuff grows crazy around here.  Sometimes you can just ask a person whose yard it is growing in, "can I have a few poles" and most people are fine with it.  I cut it with a machete at two angles, like a V.  Stuff is rather hard on blades though, because of the outer layer which is very silica rich.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is indeed mate - but then that is up to us to change isn't it?

replies.author: @patomahony9747
replies.text: We don’t realise the strength of natural materials if used in the correct fashion. Was amazed at the strength and let’s add beauty of bamboo used as scaffolding on high rise buildings. Remembering a buddy who is a scaffolder telling me one of the issues they have is rodents chewing on the structure. The solutions to that were soooo funny.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @patomahony9747 we have an obligation to either change now or leave the world left to die in the hands of our children and there children after them. Especially if we start dropping nukes.

replies.author: @patomahony9747
replies.text: @stevetobias4890 1000% agrees. Liking this right to repair movement and hoping we get back to days it was possible to repair stuff. Some weeks ago a friend had washing machine trouble,the drum bearing broke. I had a look and guess what the drum is a sealed unit. Basically have to replace the complete drum mechanism which is nearly as expensive to buy as a new machine. Sadly before I could get old machine off him to “experiment “ it was removed. Recon if I could have found a way to open/cut outer drum casing could have repaired it for a couple of quid and saved the planet lots of waste.
